LAW AND MOTION 302, PLAINTIFF VIRGIN HOTELS SAN FRANCISCO, LLC'S MOTION TO FILE RECORDS UNDER SEAL. GRANT. NO OPPOSITION FILED, AND THE MOTION COMPLIES WITH CALIFORNIA RULES OF COURT, RULES 2.550 AND 2.551. THE COURT FINDS THAT THERE EXISTS AN OVERRIDING INTEREST THAT OVERCOMES THE RIGHT OF PUBLIC ACCESS TO THE PORTIONS OF THE RECORDS TO BE SEALED; THE OVERRIDING INTEREST SUPPORTS SEALING THOSE RECORDS; A SUBSTANTIAL PROBABILITY EXISTS THAT THE OVERRIDING INTEREST WILL BE PREJUDICED IF THE RECORD IS NOT SEALED; THE PROPOSED SEALING IS NARROWLY TAILORED; AND NO LESS RESTRICTIVE MEANS EXIST TO ACHIEVE THE OVERRIDING INTEREST. COUNSEL FOR THE PLAINTIFF IS REQUIRED TO SUBMIT A PROPOSED ORDER WHICH STATES THAT THE MOTION WAS NOT OPPOSED AND WHICH SPECIFIES THE PORTIONS OF THE DOCUMENT TO BE SEALED. JUDGE: ETHAN P. SCHULMAN, CLERK: S. LE, NOT REPORTED. =(302/EPS)

Notice Of Motion To File Under Seal
Off Calendar Dec-06-2022 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Apperances:
Attorney Todd Soloway appeared for plaintiff and cross-defendant Virgin Hotels SF.
Attorney Cecelia Fanelli appeared for defendant and cross-complainant 250 Fourth.

After hearing discussions in Defendant's/Cross Complainant, Defendant/ Cross-Complainant, 250 Fourth's Motion to File Under Seal, the Court rules as follows:

250 Fourth's motion to file under seal some of its supplemental moving papers on its motion for new trial is tentatively denied due to lack of the required showing to support a sealing order. Virgin Hotels, the party who desires the sealing, asked for and was given a week to advise the court whether it wishes to file papers in an effort to make the required showing. The court will issue a brief written order.

Judge Harold E. Kahn; No Clerk Present; Court Reporter: Mike Hensley,(mike@mphsteno.com).(318)

Off Calendar Dec-06-2022 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Apperances:
Attorney Todd Soloway appeared for plaintiff and cross-defendant Virgin Hotels SF.
Attorney Cecelia Fanelli appeared for defendant and cross-complainant 250 Fourth.

After hearing discussions in Defendant's/Cross Complainant, 250 Fourth's Motion to Strike or Tax Costs, the Court rules as follows:
Granted in part and Denied in part. The costs for the depositions taken by 250 Fourth and the costs for the depositions that either party designated any excerpts for inclusion in the trial are allowed. The costs for any other depositions are disallowed. The transcript costs are disallowed. The court will issue a brief written order.

Off Calendar Dec-06-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES: Apperances:
Attorney Todd Soloway appeared for plaintiff and cross-defendant Virgin Hotels SF.
Attorney Cecelia Fanelli appeared for defendant and cross-complainant 250 Fourth.

After hearing discussions in Defendant's/Cross Complainant, Defendant/ Cross-Complainant, 250 Fourth's Motion for New Trial, the Court rules as follows:

This matter will be taken under submission as of December 2. No later than the end of the day December 1 counsel for 250 Fourth may send an email to the court with a copy to counsel for Virgin Hotels identifying, without argument, the portion(s) of the Hotel Management Agreement that 250 Fourth contends were breached by Virgin Hotels' entering into an agreement with Preferred Hotels & Resorts. The court will issue a written order by December 6.

MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF VIRGIN HOTELS SAN FRANCISCO, LLC's Motion For Leave To File First Amended Complaint.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff Virgin Hotels San Francisco, LLC's motion for leave to file first amended complaint is granted. The motion is supported by the strong policy favoring liberal amendment of pleadings. The amendment is warranted, among other grounds, to allow plaintiff to add factual allegations regarding events occurring after the original complaint was filed. Defendants do not show that they would be prejudiced by the amendment: the trial date is not until February 7, 2022, discovery has not yet commenced, and no dispositive motions have been filed. To the extent that defendants seek to attack the viability of the proposed amended complaint, including the proposed alter ego claims, they may do so by demurrer, motion for summary adjudication, or other appropriate pretrial motion. (See California Casualty Gen. Ins. v. Superior Court (1985) 173 Cal.App.3d 274, 281 ["the preferable practice would be to permit the amendment and allow the parties to test its legal sufficiency by demurrer, motion for judgment on the pleadings or other appropriate proceedings."].) The Court denies defendants' request that it order production of documents as a condition of granting leave to amend.

Order signed.

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Seal Documents And Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; ***Court Reporter Fees Paid***
Off Calendar Jun-29-2021 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF VIRGIN HOTELS SAN FRANCISCO, LLC's Motion To Seal Documents.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff Virgin Hotels San Francisco, LLC's motion to seal the parties' Technical Services Agreement is denied without prejudice. That the TSA contains a confidentiality provision by which the parties agreed that its terms are strictly confidential, and agreed to use their commercially reasonable best efforts to ensure that its terms not be publicly disclosed, is insufficient for the Court to find that the requirements of Cal. R. Ct. 2.550 are met. (See Cal. R. Ct. 2.551(a) ["The court must not permit a record to be filed under seal based solely on the agreement or stipulation of the parties"]; Universal City Studios, Inc. v. Superior Court (2003) 110 Cal. App. 4th 1273, 1282 [rejecting contention that sealing of court documents is permitted "merely upon the agreement of the parties without a specific showing of serious injury"]; see also McNair v. National Collegiate Athletic Assn. (2015) 234 Cal.App.4th 25, 36 ["the mere agreement of the parties alone is insufficient to constitute an overriding interest to justify sealing the documents"]; Huffy Corp. v. Superior Court (2003) 112 Cal.App.4th 97, 106-107 [same, declining to seal confidential settlement agreement with nonparty insurer].) Plaintiff's conclusory assertions that it will be materially prejudiced if the TSA is disclosed because such disclosure will be detrimental to it in negotiating similar agreements with hotel owner-counterparties and will permit Plaintiff's competitors to gain access to confidential deal terms and other proprietary information about the Virgin Hotels Brand (Mohler Decl. par. 7) are insufficiently specific, and are based solely on a declaration of counsel, who lacks personal knowledge and therefore is not competent to testify on such matters. (See Universal City Studios, Inc., 110 Cal.App.4th at 1282 ["In delineating the injury to be prevented, specificity is essential. Broad allegations of harm, bereft of specific examples or articulated reasoning, are insufficient." (citation omitted)].) Finally, Plaintiff's request that the entire agreement be sealed is not narrowly tailored, as required by Cal. R. Ct. 2.550(d)(4); compliance with that requirement could justify, at most, the sealing or redaction of certain financial terms in the TSA, not the entire contract. (See Huffy Corp., 112 Cal.App.4th at 107 ["Defendant has not asked to seal any particular parts of the settlement agreement. Much of the settlement agreement is routine verbiage which appears in most settlement agreements."].)

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported (302/EPS)

Off Calendar Nov-20-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Bryan Mohler (212-326-0466; BMohler@PRYORCASHMAN.com) and Meghan Hill (MHill@PRYORCASHMAN.com) of Pryor Cashman LLP for Plaintiff.
Cecelia Fanelli (clf@brewerattorneys.com) and Peter Bach-y-Rita (214.653.4036; pbr@brewerattorneys.com) of Brewer, Attorneys & Counselors for Defendant.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF VIRGIN HOTELS SAN FRANCISCO, LLC's Notice Of Application And Hearing For Right To Attach Order:

Plaintiff Virgin Hotels San Francisco, LLC's application for right to attach is denied.

Under Code of Civil Procedure section 483.010, a prejudgment attachment may issue only if the claim sued upon is (1) a claim for money based upon a contract, express or implied; (2) of a "fixed or readily ascertainable amount" not less than $500; (3) either unsecured or secured by personal property, not real property (including fixtures); and (4) commercial in nature. The plaintiff must establish "the probable validity of the claim upon which the attachment is based." (CCP 484.090, subd. (a)(2).) "A claim has 'probable validity' where it is more likely than not that the plaintiff will obtain a judgment against the defendant on that claim." (CCP 481.190; see also Loeb & Loeb v. Beverly Glen Music, Inc. (1985) 166 Cal.App.3d 1110, 1120.)

Plaintiff fails to demonstrate the "fixed or readily ascertainable amount" that it is owed and further fails to show the probable validity of the claim. Plaintiff relies on the declaration of its general counsel (Thompson). The Thompson declaration does not show personal knowledge regarding the alleged outstanding expenses and attaches numerous documents (e.g. Hotel Ledgers) without a showing that those documents meet the business records exception to the hearsay rule. The court sustains defendant's objections to exhibits D, E, F, and G to the Thompson declaration based on a lack of personal knowledge and hearsay. Plaintiff also fails to show that it can recover all of the claimed expenses. For example, plaintiff claims that it paid certain employee benefits with the "understanding" that defendant would reopen the hotel and rehire many of the furloughed employees. (Thompson Decl., par. 54.) Defendant, on the other hand, explains that it was not responsible for such payments and cites evidence showing that plaintiff agreed that defendant's payments were "discretionary." (Singh Decl., pars. 37-47; Ex. E [e-mail from plaintiff's employee Leal].) Based on this equivocal record, the court denies the application for attachment.

order signed.

Judge: Richard Ulmer, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Rose Miller, CSR#5041, email: RMillerCSR@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/RBU)

DEMURRER to 1ST Amended CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Oct-30-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Bryan Mohler of the law offices of PRYOR CASHMAN LLP (BMohler@PRYORCASHMAN.com) appears as counsel for moving party, Plaintiff

Attorney, Peter Bach-Y-Rita of the law offices of BREWER ATTORNEYS AND COUNSELORS (pbr@brewerattorneys.com) appears as counsel for opposing party, Defendant

No other appearance is made in this matter

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides, on record as reflected in the notes of the certified shorthand reporter.

The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

PLAINTIFF VIRGIN HOTELS SAN FRANCISCO, LLC's DEMURRER to First Amended CROSS-COMPLAINT, as to the five causes of action in 250 Fourth Development, LP's first amended cross-complaint (FACC) are all OVERRULED.

Fraud. The FACC adequately pleads constructive fraud. (Civ. Code 1573; CACI 4111.) The fraud claim arises in part from extra-contractual duties, and California law permits pleading claims in the alternative in any event. (Adams v. Paul (1995) 11 Cal.4th 583, 593.)

Breach of Contract. 250 Fourth pleads "written notice to Virgin Hotels of all such breaches under the HMA." (FACC 6:26-7:2.) Virgin raises disputes of fact and mixed disputes of fact and law regarding the notices. But under California law, a demurrer admits the truth of all facts alleged in a complaint no matter how unlikely. (Del E. Webb v. Structural Materials Co. (1981) 123 Cal.App.3d 593, 604.) The opinions Virgin cites do not involve demurrers and/or do not apply California procedural law.

Breach of Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing. No special relationship is needed for the implied covenant where contract damages are sought. (Foley v. Interactive Data Corp. (1988) 47 Cal.3d 654, 685-85.) Nor does this cause of action duplicate the breach of contract claim.

Breach of Fiduciary Duty. A contract provision denying an agency relationship is not dispositive; rather, "the question is one of fact for the jury." (Nichols v. Arthur Murray, Inc. (1967) 248 Cal.App.2d 610, 613; Seneris v. Haas (1955) 45 Cal.2d 811, 831.)
Accounting. A fiduciary duty is pled. It is unclear whether the right to recover a sum can be made certain by calculation and/or whether a legal remedy will be adequate; discovery may tell.

The prevailing party shall submit a [proposed] written order that tracks the Court's ruling, verbatim and that complies with CRC 3.1312.
